reasoning: The linear model from historical stock data estimates a 52% probability of the stock closing higher for the first resolution date based on upward drift, though a single seasonal data point from the prior year showed a decrease in May. Stock movements over weekly and monthly horizons follow a near random walk, so we stay anchored close to the 50% baseline. As the forecast horizon extends to August and November, the unadjusted stock price will absorb one and then two quarterly ex-dividend drops (historically around $1.09 each, occurring mid-June, mid-September, and mid-December). These dividend drops will temper the natural upward market drift of the utility stock. In keeping with the principle that short-term predictable positive drift becomes overwhelmed by noise and dividend drags over time, our uncertainty increases and the probability decays toward exactly 0.50 for the most distant resolution dates.
